refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of what 'availability' means (uptime ratio), the measurement period, and inclusion of safety-driven constraints. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety—safety-driven shutdowns must not penalize the availability metric.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold) with timestamped records. Calculate availability as (total_scheduled_time - unplanned_downtime - planned_maintenance_time) / total_scheduled_time. Validate against customer-defined baseline over agreed evaluation window (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that result in unplanned downtime within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall provide predictive maintenance alerts to reduce unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall isolate and recover from transient faults without operator intervention.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall maintain safety-critical functions even during partial system degradation.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The system shall provide real-time availability status and downtime root-cause reporting to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant hardware and software subsystems with automatic failover to mask transient faults and reduce mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
DP-B: Deploy continuous health monitoring and predictive maintenance algorithms that detect degradation early and schedule maintenance before failures occur.
DP-C: Implement modular architecture with hot-swappable components and rapid replacement procedures to minimize downtime duration.
DP-D: Combine redundancy (DP-A), predictive health monitoring (DP-B), and modular design (DP-C) with centralized availability tracking and root-cause analysis.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered availability strategy combining (1) redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) continuous health monitoring with predictive maintenance alerts, (3) modular architecture enabling rapid component replacement, and (4) centralized telemetry-driven availability tracking with root-cause analysis reporting.

potential_sources: Redundancy logic (DP-A) must be decoupled from health monitoring (DP-B) to avoid cascading false positives.
Modular replacement (DP-C) depends on standardized interfaces and rapid diagnostics (DP-B output).
Availability tracking (DP-D aggregation) depends on all subsystems reporting state via Telemetry interface.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that integrates three independent strategies. Coupling is managed through clear information flows (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and separation of concerns (redundancy logic isolated from diagnostics). No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms have sufficient historical failure data to train models.
Assumption: Operators and maintenance teams will act on predictive alerts within acceptable time windows.
Risk: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design flaws; must pair with root-cause analysis.
Risk: Predictive models may have false positive rates that erode operator trust; requires tuning and validation.
Risk: Rapid component replacement requires pre-positioned spares and trained technicians; logistics dependency.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should explicitly define thresholds for predictive alerts, failover decision logic, and the role of human operators in availability-critical decisions. Recommend adding a sub-DP for 'Availability Decision Logic' that specifies when automatic failover is triggered vs. when operator confirmation is required.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the end-to-end availability KPI, including redundancy orchestration, health monitoring aggregation, and availability reporting. The module is responsible for coordinating failover decisions across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems and ensuring that safety-critical functions remain available even during partial degradation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional telemetry stream carrying system state, health metrics, fault logs, and availability status from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to NGHE Solution aggregation layer and back to Site Manager dashboard.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts triggered by availability-impacting faults or safety-critical degradation; routed to Safety Services and Site Manager.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound task commands from Site Manager or Tele-operator; NGHE Solution validates availability state before accepting new tasks.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network connectivity for all telemetry, tasking, and alert exchanges; availability of Network Link directly impacts NGHE Solution availability.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability KPI. It aggregates health data from Vehicle and Teleoperation, orchestrates failover, and reports availability metrics. Minimal diffusion: only the Network Link dependency is external. Redundancy logic, health monitoring, and availability tracking are all contained within NGHE Solution or its direct children (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

Vehicle owns hardware redundancy and fault detection for propulsion, steering, and payload systems. However, availability KPI requires cross-system coordination (Vehicle + Teleoperation + Safety Services), which Vehicle cannot fully contain. Assigning to Vehicle would diffuse availability responsibility and create coupling with Teleoperation.

Teleoperation owns remote control redundancy and network failover. However, it does not own Vehicle hardware availability or Safety Services integration. Assigning to Teleoperation would fragment the availability KPI across multiple modules.

Safety Services owns safety-critical monitoring and shutdown logic. While safety is a component of availability (safety-driven shutdowns must not penalize the metric), Safety Services does not own the broader availability strategy (redundancy, predictive maintenance, modular replacement). Assigning to Safety Services would conflate safety with availability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the correct owner because: (1) it is the top-level system boundary that integrates all subsystems, (2) availability is a system-level KPI that cannot be owned by any single subsystem, (3) it owns the aggregation of health telemetry and the orchestration of failover decisions, (4) it has clear interfaces to all subsystems via Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking, and (5) it can enforce the availability target without diffusing responsibility.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it also owns detailed fault detection logic. Mitigation: Delegate fault detection to Vehicle and Teleoperation; NGHE Solution aggregates and orchestrates.

Risk: Network Link is external and outside NGHE Solution control. If Network Link fails, availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: Explicitly document Network Link as a dependency and include redundant network paths in the design.

Risk: Availability tracking requires synchronized clocks and consistent state logging across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Mitigation: Implement a centralized time-sync service and standardized logging format.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary. Recommend adding a sub-module 'Availability Manager' within NGHE Solution that owns the availability tracking, predictive maintenance algorithms, and failover orchestration logic. This sub-module should have clear interfaces to Vehicle (for hardware health), Teleoperation (for network health), and Safety Services (for safety-driven shutdown events).

fr_dp_gate.reason: FR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) is clearly mapped to DP-D (multi-layered availability strategy). DP-D directly addresses the requirement through redundancy, health monitoring, modular design, and centralized tracking. The mapping is one-to-one and non-circular. DP-D is implementable and measurable against the ≥0.97 availability target.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy logic (DP-A component) is distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. NGHE Solution must aggregate and orchestrate failover without duplicating logic. Mitigation: Define clear failover decision rules at NGHE Solution level; Vehicle and Teleoperation implement only local redundancy.

Predictive maintenance algorithms (DP-B component) require data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. NGHE Solution must centralize the algorithms to avoid diffusion. Mitigation: Implement predictive engine as a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution with standardized input interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution contains the availability KPI ownership and orchestration logic. Vehicle and Teleoperation contain subsystem-level redundancy and health monitoring. Safety Services contains safety-critical shutdown logic. Interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are well-defined and non-ambiguous. No module boundary conflicts detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified through continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation analysis. Measurement requires timestamped state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold) and calculation of uptime ratio. Feasible with standard instrumentation and data collection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution with clear sub-allocations to Vehicle (hardware redundancy), Teleoperation (network redundancy), and Safety Services (safety-critical availability). Each sub-allocation has measurable targets (e.g., Vehicle MTTR < 30 min, Teleoperation failover latency < 5 sec). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to explicitly define failover decision logic: when is automatic failover triggered vs. operator confirmation required? This is critical for safety-critical systems.
Add a sub-DP for 'Availability Measurement and Reporting' that specifies data collection, aggregation, and reporting mechanisms. This ensures that the ≥0.97 target is measured consistently.
Define Network Link redundancy strategy explicitly. If Network Link is a single point of failure, availability target cannot be met. Recommend dual-path network architecture.
Establish acceptance criteria for predictive maintenance false positive rate (e.g., <5%) to ensure operator trust and system credibility.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing specific availability mechanisms (redundancy, health monitoring, modular design, rapid recovery). The refined FR and sub-FRs are well-defined and atomic at the next level. DP-D is sound but composite; it should be decomposed into sub-DPs aligned with sub-FRs. NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary, but it requires sub-modules (Availability Manager, Health Monitor, Failover Orchestrator) to implement DP-D without creating a monolithic god-module. Recommend proceeding to the next decomposition level to detail the sub-FRs, sub-DPs, and sub-IMs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that result in unplanned downtime within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall provide predictive maintenance alerts to reduce unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall isolate and recover from transient faults without operator intervention.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall maintain safety-critical functions even during partial system degradation.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The system shall provide real-time availability status and downtime root-cause reporting to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

assumptions: Availability is measured as uptime ratio over an agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
Safety-driven shutdowns do not penalize the availability metric; only unplanned downtime and planned maintenance are counted.
Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Predictive maintenance algorithms have sufficient historical failure data to train models.
Operators and maintenance teams will act on predictive alerts within acceptable time windows.
Network Link has sufficient redundancy to support the ≥0.97 availability target.
Synchronized clocks and consistent state logging are available across all subsystems.
open_risks: Over-reliance on redundancy may mask underlying design flaws; must pair with root-cause analysis.
Predictive models may have false positive rates that erode operator trust; requires tuning and validation.
Rapid component replacement requires pre-positioned spares and trained technicians; logistics dependency.
Network Link is external and outside NGHE Solution control; single point of failure if not redundant.
Failover decision logic must balance availability (automatic failover) with safety (operator confirmation); unclear trade-off.
Availability measurement requires synchronized clocks and consistent logging; implementation complexity.
evidence_needed: Historical failure data for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to validate predictive maintenance models.
Network architecture design showing redundancy and failover paths.
Failover decision logic specification with safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) to validate automatic vs. operator-confirmed failover.
Availability measurement methodology and data collection plan.
Cost and schedule estimates for redundant hardware and software components.
Operator and maintenance team training plan for predictive alerts and rapid component replacement.
